turns.text: Hello Ariadna, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It is okay. Thank you for asking. I am a little tired—the baby moves so much at night, and walking the streets here in Lawrence, my feet just feel heavy, like I am carrying the whole world in front of me. This is my third time, so I know how the body changes, but this time… everything feels different. Maybe it is just because I am older, or maybe I am just more scared.

The doctors, they are nice, I think. But when I am in the clinic, I feel very small. They use these big words—*genetic screening*, *nuchal*, things I have to translate in my head while they keep talking. Sometimes the interpreter is there, but it feels like the words they say are not the ones my heart is feeling. Last week, when they talked about the tests for Down syndrome, the room felt so cold. I just looked at the paper they gave me, all those numbers, and I wanted to ask, "Will my baby be happy? Will I be able to give them everything they need?" But I didn't say it. I just nodded because I don't want to be the "difficult" mother who doesn't understand. I just want my baby to be healthy. Is that too much to ask?

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

When I first found out, I think I expected... I don’t know, I guess I expected it to be a conversation. I imagined sitting with a doctor who would look at me—not at the computer screen—and ask me how I was doing, how I was managing. I thought they would walk with me, you know? Like a partner. I wanted someone to explain things in a way that made me feel like I was doing a good job, even if my English isn't perfect.

But the reality? It’s very fast. *Rápido*. I walk into the clinic, I check in, and everything is about the paperwork. The environment feels... sterile, I guess is the word. Everything is white and bright, and the people are always rushing. They have their schedules to keep, and I understand that, but I don’t feel like I am a person there. I feel like I am just a box to be checked. 

When they talk, they give me all these pamphlets. They are full of charts and risks and percentages. I bring them home, but they don't answer the quiet questions in my head. They explain the *what* of the baby, but never the *how*—how I am supposed to do this alone, how to be strong for my other two. It isn't a conversation; it’s a delivery of data.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise? It was how lonely it felt in a room full of people. I think I expected that when I saw the doctor, they would see *me*—not just my blood pressure or my age or the fact that I am not married. I thought they would ask about my life, maybe ask how I balance the kids or how work is going. But the surprise was that my life, the life I am living right now in Lawrence, feels completely separate from the medical side of the pregnancy.

The negative part, I think, is how much they focus on "risk." Everything is about "what if something goes wrong." I understand they have to be careful, but it makes me feel like I am walking on eggshells. They look at me and they see a low-risk pregnancy, so they act like everything is "fine." But just because the baby is growing okay does not mean I am okay. 

I guess I wanted them to be a bridge, helping me feel steady. Instead, I feel like I am standing on one side of a deep canyon, and they are shouting instructions from the other side in a language I have to work so hard to translate. It’s a very heavy feeling.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I thought it would be like in the movies, maybe? I imagined a doctor sitting down, holding my hand, and looking me in the eye to say, "Everything looks good, Ariadna," or "We need to look closer at this." I thought there would be a moment for us to talk about what the results meant for my daily life. I expected someone who would care if my hands were shaking.

But the reality? It’s a portal notification on my phone. Sometimes I get an email, and I have to stare at these codes and long, strange words, trying to guess if they are good or bad before I even see the doctor again. When I do see them, they just look at the screen and say, "Everything is low risk, you're fine." 

They sound relieved, but they don't see me still sitting there, waiting for them to explain what "low risk" actually means for the baby's future. They are so busy clicking away on the computer that the human part, the heart-to-heart part, just disappears. It feels like they are reading from a script and then closing the book before I even have the courage to speak. It’s not a conversation; it’s just a report card, and I’m always just trying to pass.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Oh, yes. The gap is very wide. They give me so much paper, so many brochures with pictures of chromosomes and lines on a graph. It is like being handed a map for a city I have never visited, in a language I cannot read. They give me *information*, yes, but they do not give me *understanding*. 

When I look at the results, I am not looking for percentages. I am looking for reassurance. I want to know, "Is my baby okay? What will this mean for us?" I need someone to translate the *meaning* behind the data. But the doctors, they seem to think that if they give me the pamphlet, they have done their job. They check the box that says "Patient informed." 

But I am not informed. I am just overwhelmed. I go home and I search things on my phone, trying to find someone who explains it without using those sharp, heavy medical words. I feel like I am trying to build a house with only half the tools. I want to know more, but I am afraid to ask because I don't want to sound stupid, or slow. I just want to feel like a mother who knows what is happening with her own body, not just a patient waiting for the next test.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I truly believed that when you are pregnant, there is someone who walks the path with you from the start to the end. I thought I would have one person—maybe a midwife or one specific doctor—who would recognize my face when I walked in, who would remember that I have two little ones waiting for me at home, and who would know that my English gets a little tangled when I am nervous. I expected a relationship. You know, like someone who knows your story so they don't have to ask you the same questions every single time.

But the reality is very different. Every time I go to the clinic, it is like a lottery. I never know who I will see. It is usually someone new, someone who just flips through my chart—my file—for five seconds before they start talking. They don't know me. They don't know my worries. I have to explain myself over and over, like I am a new patient every single visit. 

I feel like a revolving door. I come in, I talk to a stranger, I leave, and they forget me the moment I walk out the door. It makes me feel like my pregnancy is just a series of appointments, not a journey with a person who cares if I reach the finish line safely.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It is exhausting, honestly. Every time I walk in, I have to mentally prepare myself to start over. I have to find the right words again, explain my history again, and try to judge if this new person is going to listen or if they are just going to rush through the exam. It’s hard to trust someone when you’ve only ever seen them for ten minutes and you know you probably won’t see them again for the next visit.

Emotionally, it makes me feel invisible. When you see the same person, you build a little bridge of trust. You can say, "I felt that pain we talked about last time," and they know exactly what you mean. But with a new face every time, I feel like I am guarding myself. I don't share my fears—not the real ones, like how I worry about being a single mom or how the bills are piling up—because why bother? They don’t know me. They are just there to measure my belly and check the numbers.

Practically, it is even harder. I feel like I am constantly correcting them or reminding them of things I already said. I am the only one keeping my own story together. I have to be my own advocate when I am already so tired, and that is not how it should be, right? I should be resting, preparing for my baby, not playing a detective with my own health records.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected the doctors to be curious about me, you know? Like, when the baby kicks or when I feel a new ache, I thought they would ask me what it feels like, or tell me, "Ariadna, this is normal, this is your body doing a beautiful thing." I wanted them to treat my body with a little bit of wonder, because even though it is my third time, it is still a miracle.

But the reality is much colder. My body feels like an object they are inspecting. They move my clothes, they press the cold jelly on my skin, they measure the size of the belly, and they write it down. It is all so technical. They touch me, but they don't *see* me. There is no, "How are you feeling in your heart today?" or "How is your back?" It is just, "Measurements are good," or "Weight is fine."

When they talk about my body, it is like they are talking about a car that needs a check-up. *Click, click, click* on the keyboard. It makes me feel a little bit disconnected from myself. Sometimes I leave the office and I feel like I need to go home and just hold my belly for a long time, just to remember that this is my baby, not just a set of statistics for their files.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one time, a few weeks ago, that I still think about when I am trying to sleep. I was having a lot of pain in my lower back—it was sharp, like a needle—and I tried to tell the doctor. But because of the language barrier, I struggled to explain *where* it was and *what* it felt like. I was using my hands to show her, and she just became very clinical, very quickly. 

She didn't ask me to sit and breathe. She just said, "We need to do an internal exam," and started moving. I wasn't ready. I wasn't prepared for her to be so direct, so fast. She didn't explain *why* she needed to do it right then, or what she was looking for. She just told me to lie back, and the nurse was there, and the room felt so small. I felt exposed, not just physically, but because I couldn't find the words to say, "Wait, please, tell me what you are doing."

I just laid there and looked at the ceiling tiles, counting them, trying to disappear. It felt like I lost my voice. I felt like a body on a table, not a person. Afterward, she just left the room, and I had to dress myself while my hands were still shaking. I left feeling small and embarrassed, and I didn't ask any more questions. I just wanted to go home.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

Well, luckily, I don’t have those things. My doctors keep telling me I am "low-risk," which they say is a good thing. But honestly? Sometimes I think I would understand better what is happening if there *was* a complication. At least then, there might be a clear plan, or a nurse who calls to check on me, or specific instructions I could follow to feel like I am doing something right.

Right now, because I am "low-risk," it feels like they don't have time for me. I see other women in the waiting room who have special appointments or extra care because of their conditions, and even though I know I should be happy that I don't have those problems, I feel a little bit jealous. They get more attention. They get more explanations. 

I worry that because I am healthy on paper, nobody is looking at the stress I am carrying in my head. I have to manage the "risk" of being a mom with two kids, working, and not speaking the language perfectly, but none of those things have a code or a blood test result that they can track. So, I just manage it alone. I expected that even a "low-risk" pregnancy would feel like being taken care of, but instead, it just feels like being left to my own devices.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

It is hard to name just one thing because it feels like a whole chain of missing pieces. If I have to say what is missing most, it is the *acknowledgment*. 

I want someone to acknowledge that my life is not just a clinic visit. I am a woman here in Lawrence, working to put food on the table, trying to keep my other children happy and safe, and then—*on top of all that*—I am growing a life. I need someone to say, "Ariadna, you are doing a lot, and it is okay to feel tired." 

Practical help is also a ghost. Everyone talks about "informed decisions," but nobody helps me with the *practicality* of them. Like, how do I get to these extra appointments when I have to find childcare or leave work? How do I navigate the system when the letters come in English? I expected that in a place as big as this, there would be someone—a navigator, maybe—whose job it was to bridge the gap between their office and my life. Instead, the burden is all on me.

Emotionally, I just wanted to feel like I mattered as a human being, not just a set of data points on their screen. I wanted to feel like they cared about the "me" behind the "low-risk" label. I don't need them to be my family, but I do need them to be my ally.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I felt something was wrong, I could just call. I imagined a nurse, or even a recording, that would guide me to a quick answer. I thought, *this is a hospital, they are the experts, they should be the first place I turn.* I expected a sense of safety, like there was a door always open for my questions.

But the reality is a maze. When I call, I reach a machine, or I have to explain my problem to a receptionist who is not a doctor and doesn't know my history. Sometimes they tell me to "wait and see," which is the scariest thing you can tell a mother. When I finally get to speak to someone, it is usually someone who has no idea who I am. 

I’ve had moments where I was truly worried—like when I felt the baby wasn't moving enough—and I didn’t want to go to the emergency room because I was scared of the bill and the chaos. I wanted to talk to *my* doctor, but I couldn't reach them. The system feels like a high wall. You have to climb it just to get a simple "don't worry." It makes you feel very small, and very much alone with your fears. It’s like the system is designed to keep people *out*, not to help them *in*.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected it to be like it was back home—where a woman doesn't go through pregnancy alone. I imagined my house being full of voices, someone to help with the other two children, someone to hold my hand when the fears about the doctors or the money became too loud. I thought the community here would be like a net that catches you when you stumble.

But the reality of living here, away from home, is different. My friends are all just as tired as I am; they are working hard, just like me, and they have their own struggles to carry. They love me, and they tell me to "stay strong," but they cannot physically be there to help with the clinic visits or the days when I am just too weak to stand. My family is far away, so I can only talk to them through a screen, and I don't want to worry them—I don't want them to know that I am crying in the bathroom or that I feel lost in these appointments.

I’ve had to be very strong on my own. It is a lonely kind of motherhood. You feel the weight of it in your bones. The "support" I get is mostly people wishing me well, which is kind, but it doesn't change the fact that when I leave the doctor’s office, I am still walking back to a life that is very heavy to carry by myself.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I honestly didn't expect the quiet solidarity I found with the other mothers at the clinic. Sometimes, in the waiting room, we lock eyes—me and another woman who also looks a little lost, or who is also struggling to understand the forms. There is a way we look at each other, a silent "I see you." Sometimes, one of them will whisper a translation for a word I didn't catch, or just give me a tired smile that says, "I know, it is hard." That was a surprise. It is not official support, but it is a small, warm candle in a very cold place.

On the other side, what didn't materialize—and this hurts to say—was the help I thought I would have from my partner. I think I had this dream, a *fantasía*, that he would be the one to translate, the one to hold my hand, the one to make sure I wasn't scared. But he is not here, not in the way I need. That part of the "support network" is just an empty space in my house. 

I expected someone to be my partner in this adventure, not just in making the baby, but in navigating the doctors and the fears. Now, I have to be the mother *and* the father *and* the navigator. It is a lot for one heart to hold.

Before labour, what did you expect creating and using a birth plan to be like — how it would be received by staff and how much it would guide your care? And what actually happened with your birth plan during labour?

I remember the pamphlet they gave me about a "birth plan." It looked so simple, like a menu where you just check boxes: *Do you want pain relief? Who do you want in the room?* I thought that if I wrote my wishes down, it would be like a contract. I believed the nurses and doctors would see it, read it, and respect it as a promise for how my baby would come into the world. I thought it was my way of having a voice when I was too tired or in too much pain to speak.

But the reality? It felt like my plan was just another piece of paper to be put in a folder. When I was in labor, the room was moving so fast, like a machine. There were people everywhere, wires, loud sounds, and nobody was looking at my paper. They were looking at the monitors, at the clocks, at their charts. I tried to say, "But I wanted to be upright," or "I wanted to wait on the medicine," but it was like speaking into a windstorm. 

They didn't hear me because they were already onto the next step of *their* plan. It wasn't my voice that mattered; it was the protocol. My birth plan didn't guide my care—it was just something I had to let go of, piece by piece, while I felt myself slipping further and further away from the control I thought I had. It made me feel that my choices didn't belong to me, but to them.

Before labour, what did you expect your pain management options to be — the range available, how they'd be offered, and how you'd be supported in deciding? And what was the reality of pain management during your labour?

I thought there would be a real, calm conversation before the pain got too strong. I expected someone to tell me, "Ariadna, these are your choices—you can try to breathe through it, or we have this medicine, or that one." I thought I would have time to weigh what felt right for me and my baby. I wanted to feel like I was in control of my own body's threshold, that they would trust me to know when I needed help.

But the reality? When the pain became the only thing in the world, the environment felt very pushy. They didn't ask me *if* I wanted something; they asked me when I was already screaming or shaking. It felt like they were pushing the epidural on me like it was the only way to get the job done quickly. I didn't feel supported; I felt like I was being herded. When you are in that much pain, it is impossible to be "informed." How can I make an informed decision when I can barely think, and the nurse is looking at me like I am the one slowing down the clock? 

I didn't feel like I had options. I felt like I had a deadline. I didn't get to choose; I just had to surrender to whatever they thought was best for their schedule. It wasn't about my pain; it was about the routine.
Were there pain management options you wanted but weren't able to access? What got in the way?

I wanted to try moving around, you know? I heard that swaying or using a ball can help with the pressure, and I wanted to be upright as much as I could. I thought that would make me feel more in control. But in that room, it felt like there was no space for that. There were so many wires attached to me—the monitor for the baby, the blood pressure cuff, the IV. It felt like I was tied to the bed.

Every time I tried to shift or get up, someone would say, "Stay still, we need to see the baby’s heart rate." It felt like the machines were more important than how I was feeling in my own body. I kept thinking, *Is my movement bad for the baby?* I didn't know, and nobody took the time to explain how I could move while keeping the baby safe. 

It felt like the hospital wasn't built for a woman to labor; it was built for a woman to be a patient. The machines were the ones in charge, not me. I wanted gentle support, maybe someone to rub my back or help me breathe, but instead, I just got instructions to stay in the bed. I felt trapped. It was the opposite of what I hoped for.

When you arrived at the birth setting — whether hospital, birth centre, or home — what did you expect it to feel like in terms of safety, comfort, and how you'd be welcomed? And what was your actual experience of the environment when you arrived?

I wanted to feel like I was arriving at a place of care, somewhere warm. I imagined that when I walked—or was helped—through those doors, someone would say, "Welcome, Ariadna. We are ready for you. You are in good hands." I wanted to feel safe, like I was entering a sanctuary for my baby’s first breath.

But the actual arrival? It was loud, chaotic, and very cold. I felt like a stranger crashing a party where no one was waiting for me. The lights were so harsh, and the noise of the monitors beeping—*beep, beep, beep*—it just made my heart race even faster. It didn't feel like a welcome; it felt like being processed. I had to give my name and my insurance information while I was trying to manage a contraction, and the person at the desk didn’t even look up from her screen. 

There was no comfort. I was shoved into a room that felt like an office. Everything was metal and plastic. I remember feeling so small, looking at those four plain walls and thinking, *Is this where my child enters the world?* It didn't feel like a birth; it felt like an emergency. I felt invisible, just another number in the hallway, waiting for someone to finally notice I was there.
What was the biggest surprise about the physical environment or the immediate atmosphere when you arrived?

The biggest surprise was how much the *machines* were the center of the room, not me. I think I expected a bed, maybe a comfortable chair, and a space where I could feel like a mother. But when I walked in, all I saw were screens with blinking lights, tall poles with bags of medicine, and wires everywhere. It felt more like a repair shop for a car than a place where a miracle was about to happen.

Everything was so cold. Not just the temperature, but the feeling of the place. The walls were grey, the floor was hard, and there was no softness anywhere. I felt like if I tried to relax, my muscles would just hit something sharp or hard. I was surprised by how much the environment didn't care about my comfort. They didn't dim the lights or offer me a place to sit; they just pointed to a hospital bed that looked stiff and clinical.

It made me realize, *this is where they control everything*. It wasn't designed for me to give birth in a way that felt natural or human; it was designed for them to watch their data. It was just so... lonely, despite all the equipment. I felt like I was in a cage of beeps.

If your birth involved an unexpected intervention or complication, what did you expect the process of being informed and involved in those decisions to look like? And how were those decisions actually made and communicated in the moment?

I had a moment where things felt very rushed—the monitor for the baby started showing something they didn’t like, and suddenly the room felt crowded. I expected that someone would hold my hand, look at me, and say, "Ariadna, the baby’s heart is acting a little tired. We need to do this or that to help. Is that okay with you?" I wanted a choice, or at least a moment to understand.

But the reality was completely different. It was like I wasn't even in the room anymore. They started talking over me, using words I couldn't understand—*c-section, forceps, vacuum*—all these sharp, heavy words flying around the bed. Nobody explained to me what was happening, or *why*. They just started moving things around me, pushing buttons, and calling for more people.

I felt like I was being carried away by a fast river, and I couldn't grab onto anything. When I tried to ask, "What is happening?", the nurse just told me to "focus on breathing" and "don't move." It wasn't a conversation; it was a command. They made the decisions *for* me, not *with* me. I was terrified. I just wanted to know if my baby was safe, but the people in the room were looking at the screens, not at me. I felt like I had completely lost my voice, and my own birth was being taken out of my hands.
Did you feel you had genuine choice and sufficient information in those moments, or did decisions feel like they happened to you rather than with you?

Oh, no—I had no choice. It wasn't a choice; it was something that was done *to* me. I think that is the hardest part to carry home in my heart, even now. When you are in that room, and your body is shaking, and everyone around you is wearing masks and talking in sharp, fast medical words, you feel like a small child who has no say in her own life.

I didn't have information. I had orders. They would say, "We have to do this now," and because I didn't know the risks or the reasons, I just had to trust them blindly. But how can you trust someone who doesn't look at you when they speak? I felt like a spectator watching a movie about someone else. I would look at the doctor’s face, searching for a sign of reassurance—just a nod or a calm look—but all I saw was the intensity of the work they were doing. 

I wasn't a mother in that moment; I was a medical case. The decisions were made in the air above my head, like I wasn't even there. I left the hospital feeling like I had been a passenger in a car that was driven by someone who didn't ask me where I wanted to go. It wasn't my birth. It was their procedure.

During labour, what did you expect the midwife or doctor present to provide in terms of emotional support and personal attention — not just clinical care? And what did you actually experience from the staff who were with you?

I expected them to be like a lighthouse, you know? When you are in the middle of a storm, you look for a light to tell you that you are not lost and that you are going to be okay. I thought the nurses or the doctor would be a presence—someone to say, "Ariadna, you are so strong," or "Look, your baby is almost here." I expected them to be human, to notice when I was scared or when I needed a drink of water.

But the reality? It was like being in a factory. The staff, they came in and out like they were changing shifts at a plant. One would come, check the monitor, say nothing to me, and leave. Another would come, check my blood pressure, say "Good," and disappear again. They were *present*, but they were not *there*. I felt like a bed number, not a mother. 

I remember looking at one nurse, searching for some warmth in her eyes, just to feel like I wasn't doing this alone. She didn't look at me; she was staring at the paper on the clipboard. It made me feel like I was invisible. There was no touch, no kind word, no "how are you doing." Just the cold, mechanical hum of the room. It was like they had forgotten that there was a person feeling all of this, not just a body.
Was there a specific moment when you felt truly seen and supported — or a moment when you felt very alone? Can you describe it?

I remember a moment, very clearly, where I felt completely alone in a room full of people. I was having a contraction that just wouldn't stop, and I was so scared. I reached out, just a little, hoping someone would touch my hand or maybe tell me that the pain would pass, that I was doing well. But everyone was busy. One doctor was typing, the nurse was adjusting the tube in my arm, and the others were talking about something—I think it was about their lunch or the next shift—in a language that sounded like a blur to me.

I felt like I was drifting away, sinking into that bed. I was crying, not even because of the pain, but because of the silence in my own head where I wanted someone to say, "Ariadna, I am here with you." It was the most isolating feeling I have ever known. I was surrounded by experts, the best equipment, the "perfect" care—but I was completely, utterly alone. 

Then, there was another nurse who came in toward the end. She didn't say much—she didn't speak my language well—but she just held my hand and looked at me, *really* looked at me, and wiped the sweat from my forehead. She didn't have to say a word. In that one second, I felt like I was a person again. I wasn't just a chart on the wall anymore. That one small, human moment... it’s the only thing I remember with any warmth. Everything else is just cold, mechanical, and lonely.

Before birth, what did you expect the immediate period after your baby was born to be like — skin-to-skin contact, who would be present, what would be done? And what actually happened in those first moments after delivery?

I dreamt of that moment, you know? I thought my baby would be placed right on my chest, skin-to-skin, warm and soft. I imagined hearing their first cry while I held them, and just breathing them in, feeling all the hard, painful work melt away in that one second of peace. I expected the room to be quiet, just me and my baby, and maybe one person to help me if I needed it. I thought it would be our first moment as a family, just the two of us finally together.

But the reality was so rushed. As soon as the baby was born, they were whisked away to a corner, wrapped up, and checked by someone else. I couldn't see them clearly, and I couldn't feel them against my skin. There were voices, beeping again, and someone saying, "Everything is okay, don't worry." But I *was* worried because I didn't have my baby. 

I felt like an empty space in the bed. They were doing their work—weighing, cleaning, poking—while I was just lying there, still shivering from the shock of it all. It felt like they took my baby away to do "science" instead of letting us be a mother and child. I didn't get that first quiet breath of them. I had to wait, and the minutes felt like hours. It wasn't the soft, beautiful beginning I had prayed for; it felt like a separation.

You know, after they finally brought the baby to me—after they finished all their measurements and their work—I was so tired, so deeply tired. I remember holding that little bundle and thinking, *They gave me back a miracle, but they took the "hello" for themselves.* That is a hard thought to live with. 

And now, looking toward this third baby, it haunts me a little bit. I know what happens in those rooms now. I know that the "birth plan" is just a piece of paper. I know that the people in the white coats are looking at the screens, not at me. I feel this weight in my chest because I want to do it better this time, but I don't know how. I don't know how to tell them, "Please, just let me be a mother for a second before you become doctors again."

I worry that if I ask, they will think I am difficult or that I don't understand the "science." But I am not asking for science; I am asking for humanity. I am just a woman in Lawrence, trying to find a way to bring my third miracle into the world without feeling like I am disappearing into their machinery. I just want to be seen. Is that too much? To be a mother, not a case file?

It is strange to think that soon, there will be another heartbeat in my home. I look at my other two, playing on the floor, and I think about the day they were born. I realize I don't really remember the *joy* of those first moments. I remember the coldness of the room, the bright, stinging lights, and the way I felt like I was being moved around like a piece of furniture by people who didn't know my name. That is the memory I have, not the sweet, quiet beauty I see in the books.

I think about this baby, and I wonder: will it be the same? Will I have to fight again just to be the one to hold my child first? I want to be brave. I want to tell the doctor, "When the baby comes, please, *let me hold them before you take them.* Let me look at them, let me smell them, let me have my moment." 

But the fear is there, sitting in my stomach. What if they ignore me? What if they say, "No, it's procedure"? I don't want to be a nuisance, but I don't want to feel that same hollow feeling of being left out of my own miracle. I feel like I am holding my breath, waiting for the day to come, hoping that this time, someone will just look at me and realize that a mother’s heart needs as much care as a baby’s heart. I just want to feel like a mother, not just a visitor in a hospital room. That is all I want.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

It is hard to talk about. In my life, things have not always gone the way I hoped. When I think about this pregnancy, the pressure did not come from a partner being there to *insist* on things, but rather the pressure of his absence. When you are alone, the pressure to be "perfect" is much, much heavier. You feel like you cannot afford a single mistake because there is no one else to hold the pieces if you fall.

There were times, earlier on, when I wanted to wait—to make sure I had more stability, maybe a better place to live, or to feel more settled in my job before adding another child to the mix. But then, life happened. And the pressure wasn't just about what *he* wanted; it was the pressure of the world telling me, "You are a single mother, you should have known better," or "You are not prepared." 

I felt like I had to prove I could do it all perfectly to show the clinic, the social workers, and even myself that I am a good mother, regardless of my situation. It meant I pushed myself harder at work, pushed myself through the appointments without complaining, and stayed quiet when I should have spoken up. I felt I had to be "easy" for the system, because if I was "difficult," they would look at me and judge me more harshly. It is a lonely kind of pressure, feeling like you are constantly performing just to be treated with a little bit of respect.

I think, in the end, that is what scares me the most about this next birth. I have been playing this part—the "good" patient, the one who nods, the one who takes the brochures without asking too many questions, the one who doesn't complain about the cold or the hurry. I do it because I think it keeps me safe, because I think it keeps them from looking at me like a problem. But I realize now that by being "good," I have been making myself smaller.

When I hold this baby, I want to know that I fought for us. I want to feel that when the time comes to walk into that room, I can stop being the quiet one. I want to be able to say, "Stop. Let me hold my baby. Let me breathe." 

I just hope that when I do—if I can find the courage to do it—that they hear me. Not the medical part of me, not the "low-risk" part, but the mother. I’m tired of being a box to be checked. I’m tired of the silence in my own heart during the most important day of my life. I hope this time, I can be more than just a case file. I hope I can be a mother who is actually present at her own miracle. Do you think they will listen? Or am I just dreaming?
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Yes, they talk about my weight like it is a math problem they are trying to solve. Every single visit, I step on the scale, and the nurse—who doesn't know me, who doesn't know what I eat or how I work—tells me, "You are gaining a little too much, Ariadna. You need to watch your diet." 

It lands like a heavy, cold stone in my stomach. They don't ask what I eat. They don't know that sometimes, after a long shift, a quick meal is all I can manage for my other children. They don't know the stress of the grocery store or the cost of the good food they tell me to buy. They just look at the chart, look at the number, and make me feel like I am failing the baby.

It feels like they are policing my body. I am already so tired, and I am trying so hard to keep everyone healthy, but when they say those things, I feel like a bad mother before the baby is even here. It makes me want to shrink, to apologize for taking up space in their clinic. They treat my body like it’s failing a test, but they never once ask, "How are you doing, Ariadna? Is it hard to eat well right now?" 

It is just another layer of the "low-risk" box. I’m just a number that is slightly off the mark. They don't see the woman. They only see the weight.

I think the hardest part is that I leave the clinic feeling like I have to apologize for existing. When the doctor points to the chart and says, "A little much, Ariadna," I want to ask them, "Do you know what my day looks like? Do you know that I am the only one carrying all of this?" But I don’t. I just look at the floor.

It feels like my body is being judged by people who don't know my story. They talk about "healthy habits" as if they are things you can just buy at the store, like a carton of milk. They don’t see that for me, sometimes, a full belly for my children is a victory, even if it’s just pasta or something simple. They don't see the struggle in the quiet moments. 

I’m trying so hard to grow this third baby, to keep my other two fed and loved, and to work enough hours so we have a roof over our heads. When they comment on my weight, it’s not just a medical note—it feels like a judgment on how I am surviving. It feels like they are saying, "You aren't doing it right." And that makes me feel very, very alone. I’m a mother, not a math problem. I just wish, just once, they would ask about my life instead of just checking the scale. It would make such a difference.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

When I am scared, I don’t call the doctor first. I call my mother, even if she is so far away. Sometimes, I can only hear her voice through a grainy connection, but when I tell her, "Mama, I am worried about the baby," or "My back hurts so much," she says the things the doctors never do. She tells me I am doing a good job, she prays for me, and she reminds me that my body knows what to do because women in our family have done this for generations. That is the comfort I need—the reminder that I am not just a patient.

I also have a friend, Elena, who lives in my building. She has three children too. When I get those brochures from the clinic with the big, scary words, I don’t ask the receptionist what they mean. I take them to Elena’s kitchen table. We sit there, and she helps me piece together what they are trying to say. She tells me, "Ariadna, this is normal, don’t listen to them too much," or "This test, it is just for them, you don't have to be so worried."

These people are my real care team. They see *me*. They know I am tired, they know I am working hard, and they don't look at my chart; they look at my face. They don't give me stats; they give me hope. That is where I find the strength to keep going back to the clinic, even when I dread it. They are the ones who make me feel like I am a mother, not just a problem to be solved. Without them, I think I would have disappeared a long time ago.

You know, I sometimes wonder if the doctors realize that their white coats and their computers are not the only things helping me get to this due date. They think they are the ones "managing" my health, but my health is also held together by Elena’s coffee, my mother’s voice on the phone, and the way my other children look at me when they need their mama. If I only had the clinic, I think I would have broken already.

I think the biggest thing I’ve learned—and it’s a sad thing to realize—is that you can have all the medicine in the world, and all the best equipment, but if you don't feel seen, you feel like you are doing this alone. I want to tell the doctors: "You are the experts on the baby, but I am the expert on my life." 

It’s almost like I’m living two different lives. In one, I am a patient with a "low-risk" status and a weight issue and a chart that says *non-English speaker* at the top. In the other, I am a mother who is just trying to make sure that when this baby arrives, there is love waiting for them. The gap between those two lives is where all the fear lives. I just wish, just for one appointment, they would close that gap. Just one smile, just one real question—"How are *you* doing, Ariadna?"—it would change everything. It would make me feel like I’m a human being again, not just a set of instructions. Is that ever going to happen, do you think? Or am I always going to be just a checkbox?
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I can count on one hand the number of times I felt my input was actually sought, and even then, it felt like they were asking me to choose between things I didn't fully understand. Most of the time? I am just told what is happening. "We are doing this test," "You need this supplement," "Come back on this date." It is always a statement, never a question.

When they ask, "Do you have any questions?", it feels like a test I am expected to fail. If I say "no," they leave immediately. If I say "yes," they look at their watch and start explaining things in such a complicated, rushed way that I feel I am wasting their time. So, I nod. I say, "No, I am okay." And then I go out to my car and I cry, or I call Elena, or I search the internet for hours trying to find a human being to explain what they just did to me.

There was one doctor—not my usual one, just someone I saw once—who actually sat on the stool and asked me, "What are you most worried about today?" I almost started crying right there. It was so simple. Just that one question. I told her about the back pain and how scared I was about the birth. She didn't have all the answers, but she *listened*. That was the only time I felt like a participant instead of a spectator. Every other time, it feels like I am just being carried along by their process, whether I agree with it or not. I’m not a partner; I’m just luggage.

It is a strange feeling, being a passenger in your own pregnancy. You try to tell yourself that they are the experts, that they have the medical degrees and they know how to keep you and the baby safe. But when you are sitting in that clinic, watching them look at their screens instead of at you, you start to wonder if they have forgotten that this baby is a *life*, not just a sequence of tests and results.

I’ve had moments where I wanted to say, "Stop. I am scared. Tell me that this is okay." But I don't. I feel like if I am "difficult," if I ask for too much, if I take up too much of their time with my questions or my broken English, they will treat me even colder. So I swallow the fear. I make myself small. I play the part of the "good" patient because I am terrified that if I don’t, I will lose whatever little support they are giving me. 

But I carry that fear home every single day. I worry about what will happen during the birth. Will I be able to find my voice then? Or will I be even smaller, even quieter, while they do whatever they think is best for their charts? I just want to be able to say, "I am a mother. This is my child. Please, look at me." I hope that isn't too much to ask. Is it?

I think about it a lot—that moment when I’m finally at the hospital again. I hope that this time, I can find the courage to say, "No, wait. Talk to me." But the truth is, I’m scared that even if I find the words, they won’t be the *right* ones. 

Sometimes, I practice it in front of the mirror, in my small apartment, while the kids are asleep. I whisper, "Please, help me understand," or "Tell me why we are doing this." But then I think of the cold, fast, busy rooms at the clinic, and I feel my heart tighten. I worry that if I ask, they’ll just use more big, heavy words that I won’t understand, or they’ll look at me with that same impatient look that says, "We don't have time for this."

It feels like my voice is a tiny bird trapped in a cage made of their rules and their schedules. I just want to fly, you know? I want to feel like my questions have value, and that my fears are not just "low-risk" nonsense. I want to be someone who matters in that room. I hope that someone—maybe a nurse, maybe the doctor, maybe you—is listening to this. Because if I can just say it out loud, maybe it will be easier to say it when it actually counts. I’m just waiting for the day I don’t feel like I have to apologize for wanting to know what is happening to my own body and my own baby. That’s all. Just to be a person again.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every time I step through those automatic doors, the silence next to me is louder than the, *beep, beep, beep* of the machines. There is a specific kind of heaviness when you are sitting in the waiting room and you see the other women—the ones with partners sitting beside them, holding their hands, looking at the same screens, asking the questions I’m too tired to even think of. They have someone to lean on, someone to remember the doctor’s instructions, someone to share the worry with. 

I don't have that. When the doctor says something complicated, I have to be the one to catch the words, translate them in my head, and decide in a split second if I should nod or ask for help. It’s like trying to run a race while carrying a heavy bag, and no one is there to help me hold the straps. 

I remember the anatomy scan, looking at the screen and seeing the baby move, and I turned to say something—some small, happy thing—and realized there was no one there to see it with me. That’s when the isolation really hits. It isn’t just about the "medical" part. It’s about the fact that I am carrying this whole life, this whole future, and I am the only one who knows exactly how heavy it feels. The absence of a partner isn't just an empty chair; it’s an empty space in my heart where the support should have been. I have to be strong enough for two, and some days, I don’t know if I am.

Sometimes, I catch myself looking at the other women in the clinic—the ones with their partners, chatting about baby names or laughing at a joke, and I feel this sharp, stinging jealousy. It’s not that I want their life, exactly; I just want to know what it’s like to *share* the weight. When I go to my appointments, I am the one taking the notes. I am the one remembering the dates. I am the one trying to translate the doctor’s fast English into something that makes sense for my life. 

It makes the appointments feel like a test I’m constantly failing, even when the baby is healthy. I leave feeling more tired than when I walked in, because I had to be the parent, the patient, and the interpreter all at once. 

I’ve had to learn to be very brave, but being brave is exhausting. I just wish there was someone to tell me, "Ariadna, you are doing a good job," instead of just telling me what the blood pressure results were. I want to feel that when this baby finally takes their first breath, there is at least one person who knows how much I had to climb just to get to that moment. I am just a mother, and I am so, so tired of being a mother all by myself.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every night, when the house is finally quiet, that is when I go to my phone. I don't look for things like "what is a fetal monitor" or "how to read a blood pressure chart." I already have the papers for that, and they don't help. 

I look for other women's stories. I search for things like, "How did it feel when the doctor wouldn't listen?" or "What do I do when I feel like I'm losing my voice in the delivery room?" I am looking for someone who has walked this road and didn't feel like a statistic. I am looking for the "how"—not the medical *how*, but the human *how*. How do you stay calm when the room is loud and scary? How do you ask for a moment of quiet without being told you are a problem?

The doctors give me facts, but the internet—sometimes—gives me a mirror. I find blogs or videos of mothers sharing the parts of birth that the brochures leave out. They talk about the loneliness, the feeling of being invisible, the terror of not being in control. When I read their words, I realize I am not the only one. I am not "difficult" or "slow" because I am scared. I am just a mother who wants her baby to come into a place that feels like home, not a laboratory. I look for the truth that the clinic is too busy, or too sterile, to tell me.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that stays with me, more than any test result or any appointment, is the feeling that I am just a visitor in my own life. When you are pregnant, the world keeps telling you that this is the most beautiful, most important thing you will ever do—and I believe that. I look at my belly and I know this is a miracle. But the way I am treated makes me feel like I am just a bystander, waiting for other people to tell me what happens next.

I want the doctors and the nurses to know that when they see a woman like me—someone who works hard, who struggles with the language, who is alone—they are not just seeing a body to be checked. They are seeing someone who is carrying a dream, and who is terrified that the system is going to crush it. 

If I could say one thing to them, it would be this: "Please, stop looking at the screen for a minute. Look at me. Just acknowledge that I am a person, that I have a heart, and that I need you to be more than a professional. I need you to be a human."

I just want this third miracle to come into a world where I feel strong, not small. I want to look back on this and remember the joy, not just the cold, hard walls of the clinic. Thank you for listening. It feels... it feels a little lighter, just to say these things out loud. Maybe, just maybe, it won't be as lonely next time.
